name: LLM-Extracted Story Features / Story-Expectation Modelling (Fong & Gui)
core_claim: Engagement with narrative content is driven not only by what has already happened in the story but by the audience's FORWARD-LOOKING BELIEFS about what is to come. The central claim — imported from economic expectation theory — is that consumer decisions (continuing to read, commenting, voting) are shaped by anticipated future developments: the emotional trajectory the reader expects, beliefs about how characters will develop, and the uncertainty/surprise around those expectations. The novel methodological move is operational: use a pre-trained LLM as a proxy for the 'average reader's imagination' to GENERATE a distribution of plausible story continuations, then extract mid-level features from that distribution. From the spread of imagined continuations you derive three forward-looking constructs that prior content-feature work could not measure: EXPECTATION (the mean feature value across imagined continuations), UNCERTAINTY (the variance across continuations), and SURPRISE (the squared difference between expected features before vs. after the next chapter is revealed). The empirical claim is that these belief-based features add explanatory power for engagement OVER AND ABOVE features extracted from the actual consumed text — amplifying marginal explanatory power by ~31% on average. The framework is deliberately genre-agnostic, capturing broad story dynamics (anticipated emotional arc, character development beliefs, narrative pacing) rather than genre-specific plot tropes.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Hybrid, leaning EXPERIENTIAL / anticipatory rather than descriptive-topical. It does not classify what a story is ABOUT (no topic taxonomy, no settings, no plot categories). Instead it models the felt, forward-looking experience of consuming a narrative in time: what the reader expects to feel next (anticipated valence/arousal), how uncertain they are, and how surprised they end up. Three distinct levels actually coexist in the framework: (a) experiential-affective — the anticipated emotional trajectory (valence and arousal expectations); (b) thematic/psychological — 25 positive-psychology themes (e.g. personal growth, resilience) treated as expected psychological content; (c) structural-dynamic — 'semantic path' features (pacing/speed, volume of narrative ground covered, circuitousness) that describe the SHAPE of how the story moves through semantic space. So it occupies the affective + structural-dynamic dimensions simultaneously, with the unifying twist that every feature is measured in EXPECTATION and SURPRISE terms, i.e. as a property of the reader's evolving beliefs rather than of the static text. This is its differentiating value for HelpME2C: it captures 'what the audience anticipates feeling, and the violation of that anticipation' — a dimension invisible to both topical theme tags and static affect labels.
key_dimensions: Three feature SETS, each measured in three BELIEF modes (expectation = mean, uncertainty = variance, surprise = squared change). (1) EMOTION features: valence (positive/negative) and arousal (excitement/calm), extracted via pre-trained RoBERTa emotion models — gives anticipated emotional trajectory. (2) PSYCHOLOGICAL THEMES: 25 themes derived from positive-psychology literature (personal growth, resilience, connectedness, etc.), extracted via guided LDA — gives beliefs about character/thematic development. (3) SEMANTIC PATH features: speed (pacing — how fast the story moves), volume (how much narrative ground is covered), and circuitousness (how directly vs. windingly the plot progresses), computed on word embeddings — gives expected narrative dynamics. The candidate cross-cutting axes for our vocabulary are therefore: anticipated_valence_trajectory, anticipated_arousal, expectation_uncertainty (ambiguity about where the story is going), narrative_surprise (degree to which the next beat violates expectation), pacing/speed, and circuitousness (predictable vs. twisting). Critically, these are DERIVED, second-order features (mean/variance/delta over imagined futures) — not labels you read directly off the text.
cross_cultural_validity: Effectively UNTESTED cross-culturally; the empirical work is English-only and Western-platform-bound. The dataset is exclusively English-language Wattpad books (non-English books dropped at the <73%-English-words threshold), and no cross-cultural or multilingual validation was performed. [uncertain] Two distinct cross-cultural risk layers exist: (a) the METHOD (LLM imagines continuations -> extract expectation/uncertainty/surprise) is in principle language-agnostic and could be re-run on any language an LLM handles well, but its fidelity as a proxy for the 'average reader' degrades for languages and narrative traditions underrepresented in LLM training data, where imagined continuations would over-fit Anglophone tropes; (b) the THEME layer borrows a 25-item Western positive-psychology taxonomy (growth, resilience, etc.) that carries individualist assumptions and would not necessarily capture what counts as meaningful character development in other narrative cultures. The emotion (valence/arousal) and semantic-path (pacing/circuitousness) layers are more plausibly culture-portable than the psychological-theme layer. Net: the structural insight (expectations drive engagement) is likely general; the specific extraction pipeline is currently Western/English-centric and unvalidated elsewhere.
empirical_grounding: Comparatively strong and behaviourally grounded — a key differentiator from most frameworks in this corpus, which validate against self-report. This is validated against REAL engagement behaviour at scale: 30,258 chapters across 1,735 Wattpad books (collected July 2024; books published from January 2022 onward, deliberately post-dating LLM training cutoffs to limit leakage). Three real behavioural engagement metrics: continue-to-read rate (next-chapter reads / current reads), comment-to-read rate, and vote-to-read rate. Evidence type is CORRELATIONAL/predictive (regression-style marginal explanatory power), not causal-experimental: the belief-based features add ~31% average marginal explanatory power on top of actual-content features, with notable per-combination gains (emotion + voting +44%, psychological themes + voting +38%, semantic path + commenting +41%). A robust, directional finding: EXPECTED negative valence correlates with higher engagement across all three metrics — stronger than current-chapter valence alone, i.e. anticipating that something bad is coming drives people to keep reading. To validate OUR LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework we would replicate the loop: generate N continuations from a synopsis/episode, compute expectation/uncertainty/surprise features, and correlate against held-out engagement (completion, rewatch, joint-watch). The framework itself is the validation template — it is essentially a recipe for empirically grounding LLM-extracted narrative features against behavioural data.
operationalisability: HIGHLY operationalisable — this is, by design, an LLM-extraction pipeline, which is exactly why it is flagged as a direct template for HelpME2C. Pipeline: (1) recursively summarise prior chapters (GPT-4o-mini) to build context; (2) prompt an LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo, temperature 1.0) to generate ~10 distinct imagined continuations, each as ~20 bullet-pointed plot developments; (3) run off-the-shelf extractors over each continuation (RoBERTa for emotion, guided LDA for themes, embedding paths for semantics); (4) aggregate to expectation (mean), uncertainty (variance), surprise (squared delta vs. realised next unit). EXTRACTION UNIT is the CHAPTER (a temporal slice with everything-before as context) — NOT the whole work. This is the crucial architectural caveat for us: the method is fundamentally SEQUENTIAL and requires the actual unfolding text to compute surprise, so it does not map cleanly onto a spoiler-free one-shot synopsis. From a synopsis alone you can extract a coarse single-shot version (imagine continuations from the premise, take expectation + uncertainty) but you CANNOT compute realised surprise without consuming the work in order. Failure modes the authors flag: (a) temperature >1.0 produces incoherent continuations; (b) genre-trope CLUSTERING — imagined continuations over-represent common patterns and may not match real reader anticipation; (c) LLM bias against underrepresented genres/styles skews predictions; (d) a single model is a crude proxy for the diversity of human imagination; (e) text-only — blind to visual/audio, an explicit limitation for TV/film. For us: the anticipation-from-premise and uncertainty signals are extractable from a synopsis; the surprise signal requires sequential content and is not synopsis-extractable.
motivation_served: Maps most directly to a curiosity / suspense / anticipation motivation — the drive to resolve uncertainty about what happens next (hedonic in the 'page-turner' sense, but mechanistically it is about information-gap and expectation-violation rather than pleasure per se). The strong 'expected negative valence drives engagement' result ties it to suspense, dread, and morbid curiosity — wanting to find out how a feared outcome unfolds. It is largely silent on eudaimonic appreciation, mood-repair, identity, or social motivations; its lane is the temporal pull of an unfolding story. For GROUP viewing it offers a different lever than affect-match frameworks: rather than matching two people's taste profiles, it characterises a WORK by how strongly it generates anticipation/uncertainty/surprise — useful for a shared pick because a high-anticipation, high-uncertainty work is broadly engaging across viewers and gives two people a shared 'what happens next' hook to discuss. It does not, on its own, model how two viewers trade off motivations; it is a work-engagement-potential signal that a group recommender could use as a tie-breaker (pick the work most likely to keep BOTH hooked) rather than a compatibility axis.
receiver_dependence: Conceptually RELATIONAL (work x viewer), but operationally collapsed to a WORK-LEVEL proxy. The constructs — expectation, uncertainty, surprise — are inherently properties of a specific reader's evolving belief state: surprise only exists relative to what THAT reader anticipated, and anticipation depends on the reader's genre literacy and prior exposure. The framework openly acknowledges this (the LLM is a stand-in for the 'average reader', a single-model approximation of diverse human imagination). In practice it absolutizes the relational quantity by using one LLM as a universal average reader, yielding a work-level (actually chapter-level) score that is treated as if reader-independent. For a two-person recommender this distinction matters sharply: the RAW dynamics (pacing/speed, circuitousness, how much the plot can plausibly branch) are close to absolute work properties; but EXPECTATION and especially SURPRISE are receiver-dependent — a twist is only surprising to someone who hasn't internalised the genre's conventions, so a seasoned viewer and a newcomer experience different surprise from the same work. If we adopt this, we should encode the LLM-derived expectation/uncertainty/surprise as a work-level PRIOR (the 'average viewer' reading) and treat per-viewer genre-familiarity as a separate modifier — not bake reader-relative surprise into a static tag.
blind_spots: (1) Sequential dependency — requires the actual ordered text to compute surprise; not a one-shot synopsis method, so it does not directly serve a pre-watch recommender working from descriptions. (2) Text-only — explicitly blind to visual/auditory storytelling (cinematography, music, performance, editing), which is where much of TV/film's emotional trajectory and surprise actually lives; a major limitation for our medium. (3) Content-agnostic about TOPIC — like affect frameworks, it tells you anticipated feeling and dynamics but nothing about subject matter; must ride on top of a topical taxonomy. (4) Single-LLM-as-average-reader erases reader heterogeneity and bakes in LLM training biases (genre trope over-representation, weakness on underrepresented genres). (5) Validated on amateur serialised long-form web fiction (Wattpad) with chapter-by-chapter release — engagement dynamics there (cliffhanger-driven continue-to-read) may not transfer to binge-released prestige TV or films watched in one sitting. (6) Captures anticipation/curiosity strongly but is weak on appreciation, catharsis, comfort, nostalgia, rewatchability, and aesthetic pleasure — engagement here means 'keep going / comment / vote', not 'find it meaningful'. (7) Correlational, not causal — high-engagement features are identified, not shown to CAUSE engagement.
vocabulary_implications: This framework is best treated as a METHODOLOGICAL template plus a small cross-cutting facet, NOT as new topical theme slugs. Concrete recommendations: (a) Add a work-level, LLM-extractable 'anticipation-profile' facet with continuous/ordinal fields: anticipated_valence_trajectory (does the story signal rising/falling/volatile emotional arc — extractable by imagining continuations from a synopsis and averaging valence), expectation_uncertainty (how branchy/unpredictable the premise is — variance across imagined continuations, extractable from synopsis), and pacing/circuitousness (predictable-linear vs. twisting — partly inferable from synopsis + genre). Flag these as PRIORS, like the hedonic/eudaimonic facet. (b) DO NOT attempt a work-level 'surprise' tag from synopsis — surprise is sequential and reader-relative; flag it as NOT extractable from synopsis alone, requiring full ordered content (episode-by-episode) plus a per-viewer genre-familiarity modifier. Surface it only if/when we ingest full transcripts or episode data. (c) Adopt the EXTRACTION RECIPE itself as our pipeline pattern: generate N LLM continuations -> extract features -> aggregate to expectation/uncertainty -> validate against behavioural engagement (this is the closest direct template in the corpus for our extraction-and-validation loop). (d) A few derived UI/explanation tags are synopsis-extractable and worth adding: 'page-turner' / 'high-anticipation', 'unpredictable' (high expectation_uncertainty), 'slow-burn-payoff' (low early arousal, high anticipated valence swing — note overlap with existing slow-burn). (e) Explicitly note: emotion + psychological-theme layers degrade for visual storytelling and for non-English content; treat the psychological-theme layer (Western positive-psychology) with caution.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Largely ORTHOGONAL to the existing slug list, which is topical/structural; this framework adds a temporal-anticipation dimension none of the slugs capture. It VALIDATES and sharpens a handful of dynamic/texture slugs by giving them a measurable mechanism: 'slow-burn' (low early arousal + high anticipated emotional payoff = exactly a low-speed, high-future-valence-swing semantic-path signature), 'nonlinear-storytelling' (high circuitousness in the semantic-path features), and arguably 'mystery-investigation' / 'whodunit' (high expectation_uncertainty + engineered surprise — these genres are anticipation machines, and the framework explains WHY they engage). It also illuminates 'time-loop' and 'chase-and-escape' as structurally high-anticipation/high-pacing forms. It does NOT validate or invalidate the topical/moral/identity clusters — it sits at right angles to them. The clear GAP it reveals: HelpME2C has texture slugs that NAME dynamic shapes (slow-burn, nonlinear) but no facet that QUANTIFIES anticipation, uncertainty, or pacing as graded properties an LLM can score — and no notion of expectation-violation (surprise) at all. Recommendation: keep the topical slugs unchanged; add anticipation/uncertainty/pacing as a graded cross-cutting facet, and recognise 'surprise' as a sequential signal outside synopsis-based extraction. The framework's deeper contribution to us is not vocabulary but VALIDATION METHODOLOGY — it is the template for grounding any LLM-extracted narrative feature against real engagement data.
key_sources: Fong, H., & Gui, G. (2024). 'Modeling Story Expectations to Understand Engagement: A Generative Framework Using LLMs.' arXiv:2412.15239 (Columbia Business School). FREE: abstract https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.15239 ; full HTML (v1) https://arxiv.org/html/2412.15239v1 and updated v3 https://arxiv.org/html/2412.15239v3 ; PDF https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.15239 . Also on SSRN (free): https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5053346 ; RePEc: https://ideas.repec.org/p/arx/papers/2412.15239.html ; Semantic Scholar: https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/ca2396f96c58e36881276ee9f050cac65b1b4cce ; ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/386998077 . Related (narrative-driven LLM recommenders, freely accessible context): Mysore et al., 'Large Language Model Augmented Narrative Driven Recommendations,' arXiv:2306.02250 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.02250); 'Large Language Models as Narrative-Driven Recommenders,' arXiv:2410.13604 (https://arxiv.org/html/2410.13604). Dataset in the core paper: 30,258 chapters / 1,735 English Wattpad books, collected July 2024 (proprietary scrape, not released).
